graph_vector_fulltext.message:
Hello! Based on the provided EU AI Act excerpt:

Initially, a tool “used to organise, optimise and structure political campaigns from an administrative and logistical point of view” is an exception to the election-influence category because its output is “not directly exposed to” natural persons. On that basis, the system would initially not be classified as high-risk under the specific rule for AI systems intended to influence election/referendum outcomes or voting behaviour.

Reclassification is triggered once it is “subsequently found to be directly influencing voter behaviour” (i.e., it is in fact being used/intended to be used to “influence the outcome of an election or referendum or the voting behaviour of natural persons” and natural persons are directly exposed to its output). Under the excerpt, such systems “should be classified as high-risk AI systems” due to risks of “undue external interference” with the right to vote (Article 39 of the Charter) and adverse effects on “democracy and the rule of law.”

Once classified as high-risk, the excerpt indicates the high-risk compliance regime applies, including (as explicitly mentioned here) being “subject to conformity assessment,” bearing “CE marking,” and being “registered in” the “EU database.” The rationale also highlights the risks high-risk requirements are meant to address in this context—“potential biases, errors and opacity”—so the system would be expected to be handled under the high-risk framework designed to mitigate those issues.

On a fundamental-rights angle, the excerpt links the risk to Article 39 (right to vote) and to impacts on democracy, the rule of law, and individual freedoms. However, the provided context does not include the specific provision that creates (or details) a “fundamental rights impact assessment” obligation (who must do it, when, and with what contents), so I can’t state the exact FRIA trigger/steps from this excerpt alone. If you share the relevant article text on the fundamental rights impact assessment, I can map it precisely to this scenario.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: An AI system is designed for political campaign logistics but is subsequently found to be directly influencing voter behaviour. Analyse: the initial classification, the reclassification trigger, the high-risk requirements that apply, and the fundamental rights impact assessment obligation.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
Hello! Based on the provided EU AI Act excerpt:

Initially, a tool “used to organise, optimise and structure political campaigns from an administrative and logistical point of view” is an exception to the election-influence category because its output is “not directly exposed to” natural persons. On that basis, the system would initially not be classified as high-risk under the specific rule for AI systems intended to influence election/referendum outcomes or voting behaviour.

Reclassification is triggered once it is “subsequently found to be directly influencing voter behaviour” (i.e., it is in fact being used/intended to be used to “influence the outcome of an election or referendum or the voting behaviour of natural persons” and natural persons are directly exposed to its output). Under the excerpt, such systems “should be classified as high-risk AI systems” due to risks of “undue external interference” with the right to vote (Article 39 of the Charter) and adverse effects on “democracy and the rule of law.”

Once classified as high-risk, the excerpt indicates the high-risk compliance regime applies, including (as explicitly mentioned here) being “subject to conformity assessment,” bearing “CE marking,” and being “registered in” the “EU database.” The rationale also highlights the risks high-risk requirements are meant to address in this context—“potential biases, errors and opacity”—so the system would be expected to be handled under the high-risk framework designed to mitigate those issues.

On a fundamental-rights angle, the excerpt links the risk to Article 39 (right to vote) and to impacts on democracy, the rule of law, and individual freedoms. However, the provided context does not include the specific provision that creates (or details) a “fundamental rights impact assessment” obligation (who must do it, when, and with what contents), so I can’t state the exact FRIA trigger/steps from this excerpt alone. If you share the relevant article text on the fundamental rights impact assessment, I can map it precisely to this scenario.
